---
title: "Plotting my next move"
jupyter: brainvomit
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

In the Summer of 2024, my lease was up. Instead of looking for an apartment, I
procrastinated by making a map of NYC's most desirable neighborhoods, which is
universally agreed to consist of three things:

1. Subways
2. Trees
3. Food

```{python}
# Shared setup. Importing streamlit (via data_loaders) flips Plotly's default
# template to a dark one, so reset it — mirrors the old st.plotly_chart(theme=None).
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "analysis")   # dev-only helpers (gitignored; not needed in CI)

import plotly.express as px
import plotly.io as pio
import geopandas as gpd

from data_loaders import (
    get_nyc_geo, get_ridership, get_nyc_stations, get_nyc_lines,
    get_nyc_mta_network, get_nyc_trees, get_nyc_restaurants, CRS_GEO,
)
from nyc_analysis import (
    build_subway_network_graph, calculate_station_centrality,
    calculate_station_ridership, calculate_combined_centrality,
    add_metric_to_hexgrid, normalize_metric,
)

# MUST come AFTER the imports above: importing data_loaders pulls in streamlit,
# which sets Plotly's default template to a dark one (= black maps). Reset it.
pio.templates.default = "plotly"

H3_RESOLUTION = 9
CENTRALITY_PERCENTILE = 0.95


def choropleth(X, color_col, color_scale=None):
    fig = px.choropleth_mapbox(
        X, geojson=X.geometry, color=color_col, opacity=0.5, locations=X.index,
        mapbox_style="open-street-map", center={"lat": 40.7, "lon": -73.95},
        zoom=10, height=600, range_color=(0, X[color_col].quantile(CENTRALITY_PERCENTILE)),
    )
    fig.update_traces(marker_line_width=0)
    fig.update_layout(margin=dict(l=0, r=0, t=0, b=0))
    if color_scale:
        fig.update_layout(coloraxis={"colorscale": color_scale})
    return fig
```

## 1 Subways

If you know me, you know that I am lazy. I am willing to walk 11 mins *max* from
my apartment to the subway. That said, if the walk happens to pass by multiple
coffee shops and the station can get me downtown in 20 mins, I may be willing to
go a little farther... clearly, even my basest instincts recognize there is some
nuance to what it means to be 'well-connected'.

A subway station can be valuable in two ways: 1) It can get you to a desirable
place, and 2) It *is* the desirable place.

Number 1 hints at some graph shenanigans going on here. If you can capture the NYC
subway system as a graph — each node a station, each edge the line(s) connecting
them — then you can calculate the eigenvector centrality of each station, which is
exactly what I did below.

```{python}
import contextily as cx
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from IPython.display import display

nyc_stations = get_nyc_stations()
nyc_lines = get_nyc_lines(nyc_stations)
ridership = get_ridership()
nyc_mta_network = get_nyc_mta_network(nyc_lines, nyc_stations, ridership)

ax = nyc_mta_network.set_geometry('geometry')[['line', 'geometry']].drop_duplicates()\
    .plot('line', legend=True, figsize=(9, 9))
nyc_mta_network.set_geometry('start_coords')[['start_coords']].drop_duplicates()\
    .plot(color='red', ax=ax, markersize=5)
cx.add_basemap(ax, crs=nyc_mta_network.crs, alpha=0.5)
ax.set_axis_off()
ax.set_title("NYC subway system represented as a graph.")
display(ax.figure)
plt.close(ax.figure)   # show once; suppress the inline backend's auto-display
```

Intuitively, the eigenvector centrality answers: is this station connected to
other stations that are themselves well-connected? It is not enough for one
station to be connected to many others — the others must themselves be
well-connected.

To measure whether the station *itself* is desirable, I use ridership. The more
people swipe in, the more desirable it is. The final centrality score is an equal-
weighted average of ridership and centrality.*

*For the nitpicky reader, I'd rather have computed a node-weighted eigenvector
centrality, but this gets me 90% of the way there out-of-the-box, and that's good
enough for me.*

```{python}
G = build_subway_network_graph(nyc_mta_network)
centrality_df = calculate_station_centrality(G, nyc_stations)
station_ridership = calculate_station_ridership(nyc_stations, ridership)
station_centrality = calculate_combined_centrality(station_ridership, centrality_df)

nyc_connectivity = nyc_stations[['station_name', 'coords']].drop_duplicates()\
    .merge(station_centrality, on='station_name')
nyc_connectivity = gpd.GeoDataFrame(nyc_connectivity, geometry='coords', crs=CRS_GEO)

X = add_metric_to_hexgrid(get_nyc_geo(), nyc_connectivity, 'centrality_metric',
                          h3_resolution=H3_RESOLUTION, smoothing_weights=[4, 3, 2, 1])

# Lossless map shrink: snap hex coords to a ~1m grid (1e-5 deg). Hexes are ~150m,
# so this is visually identical but roughly halves the geojson embedded in each
# of the 4 maps. All later maps reuse this geometry.
import shapely
X['geometry'] = shapely.set_precision(X.geometry.values, grid_size=1e-5)

station_centrality.rename(columns={'station_name': 'Station Name',
                                   'centrality_metric': 'Centrality Score'}).head(20)
```

```{python}
choropleth(X, 'centrality_metric')
```

*The centrality score is on a 0–1 scale, but because the data is highly skewed
towards Manhattan, the color range maxes out at the 95th percentile.*

Clearly, NYC proper is very well-connected. But there are pockets in the outer
boroughs that are *relatively* well-connected — even more relevant if you want to
stay away from the hustle while still getting around quickly.

## 2 Trees

Kind of insane that there's a decadal census of each sidewalk tree in NYC
(individual health, diameter, species). It excludes park trees, so this is less
about proximity to green spaces and more the overall green-ness of walking around.

```{python}
nyc_trees = get_nyc_trees()
X = X.sjoin(nyc_trees, how='left').drop('index_right', axis=1)
X['trees'] = X.groupby([X.index, 'geometry']).tree_id.transform('count').fillna(0)
cols = ['index', 'shape_area', 'shape_leng', 'geometry', 'centrality_metric', 'trees']
X = X[cols].drop_duplicates()
X = normalize_metric(X, 'trees')
X['trees'] = X[['trees']].h3.k_ring_smoothing(weights=[2, 1])['trees']
choropleth(X, 'trees', color_scale='greens')
```

*Tree counts have been normalized to a 0–1 score.*

## 3 Restaurants

I grabbed all restaurants that had an inspection between 2023–2024, plus new
restaurants not yet inspected.

```{python}
nyc_rest = get_nyc_restaurants()
X = X[cols].drop_duplicates().sjoin(nyc_rest, how='left').drop('index_right', axis=1)
X['restaurants'] = X.groupby([X.index, 'geometry']).address.transform('nunique').fillna(0)
cols2 = cols + ['restaurants']
X = X[cols2].drop_duplicates()
X = normalize_metric(X, 'restaurants')
X['restaurants'] = X[['restaurants']].h3.k_ring_smoothing(weights=[0, 3, 2, 1])['restaurants']
choropleth(X, 'restaurants')
```

## Overall score

Since the number of trees happened to distinguish residential areas well, I weigh
it twice as much as the other two to break up the Manhattan monolith.

```{python}
X['overall_score'] = (2 * X['trees'] + 0.5 * X['centrality_metric'] + 0.5 * X['restaurants']) / 3
choropleth(X, 'overall_score', color_scale='rdylgn')
```

## Summary

Honestly, I don't know how useful this was. It mostly confirmed my intuitions
about the best neighborhoods (LIC, Williamsburg, Dumbo, Forest Hills, Bushwick).
You'll also notice it inflates Manhattan, since the city proper ranks high on all
three. Maybe later I'll add a penalty for commerciality (Midtown is great for work
and shopping... but who wants to live there?) or affordability.

## Appendix

1. [NYC 2015 tree census](https://data.cityofnewyork.us/Environment/2015-Street-Tree-Census-Tree-Data/uvpi-gqnh/about_data)
2. [NYC 2023 ridership](https://data.ny.gov/Transportation/MTA-Subway-Origin-Destination-Ridership-Estimate-2/jsu2-fbtj)
3. [NYC subway stations shapefile](https://data.ny.gov/Transportation/MTA-Subway-Stations/39hk-dx4f/about_data)
4. [NYC subway lines shapefile](https://geo.nyu.edu/catalog/nyu-2451-34758)
5. [NYC borough boundaries](https://data.cityofnewyork.us/City-Government/Borough-Boundaries/tqmj-j8zm)
6. [NYC restaurant inspections](https://data.cityofnewyork.us/Health/DOHMH-New-York-City-Restaurant-Inspection-Results/43nn-pn8j/about_data)
